policy_statement: By 2030, require all water-related public infrastructure projects to evaluate protection of population/land (e.g., river works) and prioritize urban/rural human water consumption demands in their areas of influence (A7g).
mechanism_description: Water-related public infrastructure projects will consider, in their evaluation, protecting population and land (e.g., through river works) and prioritizing support for urban and/or rural human water consumption demand in their areas of influence.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (droughts/floods) → resilient water & flood-control infrastructure planning/upgrade → ↓ exposure & ↓ sensitivity → ↓ climate damages
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: public planning, appraisal, and prioritization of water-sector infrastructure projects to explicitly include population/land protection (e.g., river works) and to secure human water consumption demand (urban/rural) in the serviced area.  
(2) Mediating physical process: improved hydraulic capacity and reliability (e.g., flood conveyance, bank stabilization, storage, redundancy, intake protection, distribution robustness) reduces failure during extremes and maintains service levels during drought/flood events.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: decreased disruption of drinking-water supply and reduced flood impacts on communities and land, lowering vulnerability and damages under climate change.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing climate hazard impacts (water scarcity and flooding) on people and assets by prioritizing resilient water supply and flood protection in infrastructure planning.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: addresses drought/flood risks by changing infrastructure planning and investment to maintain water supply and protect land/people—this is primarily about reducing hazard impacts.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while some water projects can affect energy use or methane (mitigation/resource efficiency), the described mechanism explicitly targets protection and prioritized human consumption reliability, which is an adaptation objective.  
3) Edge cases applied: if specific projects (e.g., energy-efficient pumping, leak reduction) were the centerpiece, resource efficiency/mitigation could be material; however, the canonical mechanism here is resilience prioritization for supply and flood protection, so adaptation dominates.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any policy that requires evaluating flood/river protection and prioritizing human water demand in water infrastructure decisions is inherently aimed at maintaining function under climate extremes and reducing damages, i.e., adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., flood management vs. drought resilience vs. governance/planning instrument) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could co-deliver mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., reduced emergency pumping energy, avoided reconstruction emissions), but these are generally secondary to the primary goal of reducing vulnerability to drought/flood impacts via resilient infrastructure planning.
key_indicators: “By 2030”; “all water-related public infrastructure projects will consider in their evaluation”; “ability to protect the population and land (through river works)”; “support as a priority the demands of… human water consumption”
co_benefits: Improved general water-service reliability and equity of access (urban/rural), land-use protection benefits not explicitly framed as climate-specific.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a planning/strategy requirement embedded in public investment appraisal; however, the phrasing “all… will” functions as a mandate rather than nonbinding guidance.
